evidence: `goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go` defines the canonical StarTram error message as `startramAPIConnectionErrorMessage = "Unable to connect to API server"` inside contract metadata.
`goseg/startram/errors.go` redefines the same literal as exported `APIConnectionErrorMessage = "Unable to connect to API server"` and separately uses it when wrapping errors.
`APIConnectionErrorContract()` already exposes the contract descriptor, but `startram` consumers can still depend on `APIConnectionErrorMessage` instead of that descriptor-derived contract message.
The two values are in different packages (`protocol/contracts` and `startram`) with no shared source-of-truth linkage, creating implicit duplication drift risk.

A broad pass-through seam pattern is used across orchestration and control layers (e.g., `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, `goseg/main.go`, `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, and `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`) where runtime structs mostly forward to other functions without adding policy or translation.

Multiple indirection layers are introduced before reaching concrete behavior (for example, `runtimeSeams() -> runtimeSeamRegistry -> defaultRuntime*Ops -> config/docker/container functions` in orchestration startup paths).

The code shows repeated constructor/helper families that mirror each other across modules instead of a shared helper or explicit interface contracts (runtime context/container/container-ops patterns are repeated rather than composed around one consistent boundary abstraction).
abstraction_fitness.issues_preventing_higher_score: Abstractions are used intentionally and are useful for tests, but the same pass-through seam shape is repeated across many modules. A consistent shared seam pattern (or consolidation of duplicated runtime-constructor layers) would raise leverage and reduce cognitive overhead; this is a design-smoothed debt rather than a functional defect.

evidence: README declares explicit runtime contract gate checks for changed files in this surface area, but those checks are externalized in CI/contract scripts rather than being locally self-evident in these contract/error files.
No in-file test scaffolding is present in `protocol_contracts.go`, `startram_contracts.go`, or `errors.go`; validation depends on broader package-level tests and CI scripts.
test_strategy.issues_preventing_higher_score: The contract governance contract remains CI-script-driven and not codified as in-module invariants in these files, creating weaker local test discoverability for future edits.

evidence: goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime_handlers.go and goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go duplicate transition side-effect flow patterns (persist config + cleanup + optional container restart) across several functions instead of centralizing behavior

goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go and goseg/broadcast/state.go both keep package-level runtime state as process-wide singletons/channels (`eventBus`, `healthLoopRunning/healthLoopStopFn`, `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime`) and operate directly against those globals

goseg/structs/configs.go and goseg/config/config_update_schema.go each define config behavior via manual per-key registration/switch handling, creating repeated and disconnected field-mapping responsibilities
`goseg/config/config_state.go:RandString` swallows entropy-generation errors, logs a generic warning, and returns an empty string.
`goseg/auth/tokens/tokens.go:CreateToken` uses `RandString` for id/secret/padding and proceeds without verifying non-empty entropy outputs.
Errors are effectively hidden at token-generation boundaries and converted into potentially valid but degraded token material.

findings.summary: StarTram API-connection error message text is duplicated across contract and module packages, creating a brittle coupling point.
evidence: `startram_contracts.go` declares `startramAPIConnectionErrorMessage = "Unable to connect to API server"` for the contract descriptor.
`errors.go` separately exports `APIConnectionErrorMessage = "Unable to connect to API server"` in `startram`.
`wrapAPIConnectionError` builds masked error text from the local constant while contract metadata is sourced independently.
findings.suggestion: Define a single source of truth for this message in the contract layer and have `startram` consume it directly (e.g., via the exported descriptor/message accessor), then remove the duplicate literal in `startram/errors.go` to prevent contract-message drift.

findings.summary: Duplicate StarTram API connection message literals split contract and runtime ownership
evidence: `goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go` defines `startramAPIConnectionErrorMessage` as `"Unable to connect to API server"` inside `ContractDescriptor{... Message: ...}`.
`goseg/startram/errors.go` defines a separate exported `APIConnectionErrorMessage` with the same value and uses it in `wrapAPIConnectionError`.
The two modules independently own the same user-visible contract message, so changing one without the other can desynchronize masking and contract metadata.
findings.suggestion: Remove the duplicate constant by deriving the masked message from the contract descriptor (`APIConnectionErrorContract().Message`) or by exporting/centralizing a single message value from `protocol/contracts` and consuming that in `startram/errors.go`; add/retain tests to pin the cross-file parity.

findings.summary: Ship transition handling duplicates config mutation + cleanup + restart workflows instead of a shared transition intent pipeline, so policy drift can diverge between handlers and runtime builders.
evidence: `runTogglePowerTransition` (shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime_handlers.go) performs config persistence, optional state mutation, cleanup/restart sequencing in one flow.
`buildToggleDevModeSteps` (shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go) repeats the same persist -> `urbitCleanDelete` -> start pattern.
`buildRebuildContainerSteps` and `buildRuntimeConfigIntSteps` further re-express the same state-update lifecycle with only small param differences.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single typed transition execution pipeline (e.g., `TransitionPlan` + policy object) that normalizes pre/post hooks (`persist`, `cleanup`, `restart`) and is reused by both handler-layer and builder-layer transition construction.

findings.summary: Global mutable runtime state in subsystem and broadcast modules makes lifecycle management cross-call and cross-test dependent, reducing determinism and reuse.
evidence: `subsystem_events.go` stores operational state globally (`eventBus`, `healthLoopRunning`, `healthLoopStopFn`, `dockerTransitionFailureLimit`, etc.) and lifecycle control functions (`StartDockerHealthLoops`, `StopDockerHealthLoops`) mutate that shared state.

`broadcast/state.go` keeps default runtime in `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` and resolves runtime through global package accessors (`DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `resolveDefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`).
Both files expose behavior that can only be driven through global mutable variables instead of explicit runtime handles.
findings.suggestion: Replace package globals with explicit runtime containers (injectable `subsystemRuntime` and `broadcastStateRuntime`) and pass them through start/bootstrap APIs so tests and multiple instances can run with isolated state.

findings.summary: Config binding is implemented as separate manual field-by-field switch maps in multiple modules, creating low-coherence schema ownership and duplicate maintenance paths.
evidence: `UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` in `structs/configs.go` contains a long switch over field strings with repeated parse + assignment logic.

`config_update_schema.go` introduces separate patch-field plumbing (`confPatchField`, `newConfPatchField`, parse functions) that also binds config behavior via per-key registrations and ad-hoc parsers.
Both files encode overlapping config surface concerns but are not driven from a single schema descriptor, so adding/removing fields risks inconsistent behavior across patch and JSON paths.
findings.suggestion: Define a single canonical config field metadata registry (typed descriptors with parsing, defaults, validation, and merge semantics) and generate both patch wiring and JSON unmarshalling branches from it to eliminate duplicated per-key case logic.

findings.summary: Fail-fast background service failures can be missed after startup timeout elapses
evidence: startBackgroundServicesWithRuntime checks a fail-fast service by reading from its error channel with a fixed 250ms timeout, then continues startup even if the service reports a later failure
The timeout path returns nil immediately when the first fail-fast error is not observed in-band before the timer fires
This allows startup to proceed while a required background runtime may already be in a failed state, reducing error observability and making failures non-deterministic
findings.suggestion: Start fail-fast services without a fixed short-circuit timeout, or explicitly await a readiness/health signal from the service before continuing; if readiness does not arrive within a bounded startup window, fail startup with a joined error that includes timeout root cause.

findings.summary: runServer returns only one unexpected listener error, dropping additional startup failures
evidence: server startup launches HTTP and websocket listeners and stores the first unexpected listener failure in a single `unexpectedErr` variable
When both listeners exit, the function returns only the first captured unexpected error, so additional listener failures are silently lost
Bootstrap/startup phases treat this returned error as the startup outcome, so dropped secondary failures cannot be reported or diagnosed from startup logs
findings.suggestion: Collect listener errors into an aggregate (e.g., `[]error`/`errors.Join`) while draining both listener channels, and return all collected causes with listener identity so the startup result reflects every failure.

findings.summary: Container/host command execution is handled by inconsistent pathways across modules, increasing coupling and brittle behavior.
evidence: `goseg/backups/backups.go:getVolumeFn` invokes `exec.Command("docker", "inspect", ...)` and parses text output.
`goseg/accesspoint/router.go` runs assembled shell strings via `executeShell` (`sh -c`).
Docker runtime APIs already exist in `goseg/docker/lifecycle` and `goseg/docker/orchestration` for typed orchestration.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared command runner seam in the docker/runtime layer (structured inputs and structured outputs, with timeouts and error typing) and route both accesspoint and backup Docker-host probing through it; remove direct `sh -c` and free-form parsing from module code.

findings.summary: Random token component generation suppresses entropy failures and can produce empty/weak credentials.
evidence: `RandString` logs on `rand.Read` failure but returns `""` without surfacing an error.
`CreateToken` uses `RandString` for `id`, `secret`, and `padding` and proceeds even when those values can be empty.
No explicit validation checks these security-sensitive outputs before token persistence.
findings.suggestion: Change `RandString` to return an explicit error on entropy failure and update `CreateToken` to fail token creation when any generated component cannot be produced (or retry with bounded attempts).

findings.summary: Broadcast loop lifecycle is globally singletoned, blocking independent runtime instances and increasing test coupling.
evidence: Global state in `goseg/broadcast/loop.go` (`loopRunning`, `loopStopChannel`, `loopControlMu`) controls loop lifetime across all callers.
`StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime` returns false if any loop is already running, even for alternate runtime configs.
This couples runtime behavior to process-global mutable state instead of per-runtime ownership.
findings.suggestion: Refactor loop lifecycle into a runtime-owned handle (e.g., `BroadcastLoopHandle` with explicit start/stop) so callers can manage independent loops and tests can control lifecycle without global side effects.
